Introduction
?

A binary relation defined on a set is symmetric if for all we have In other words, if a pair belongs to a symmetric relation, then the pair should also be in the relation.

For example, since implies congruence modulo over the integers is a symmetric relation.

A binary relation defined on a set is antisymmetric if for all we have In other words, only one of the pairs, either or can be in an antisymmetric relation. Note that this condition allows pairs of the form

For instance, the relation defined on the set of natural numbers, is antisymmetric since if one natural number divides another and vice versa, then those numbers must be equal.

Relations Falling Into Neither Category or Both Categories
?

Some relations are neither symmetric nor antisymmetric.

For example, defined on the set of integers, is

  • not symmetric since but and

  • not antisymmetric since and but

In contrast, some relations are both symmetric and antisymmetric!

For example, consider the relation of equality () defined over This relation contains no pairs of the form with As a result, the antecedents in the implications from the definitions of symmetricity and antisymmetricity are always false.

Therefore, the implications are always true. So, equality over is vacuously symmetric and antisymmetric at the same time!

We can summarize everything we've discussed so far in the following diagram:

Example: Identifying Symmetric and Antisymmetric Relations
?

EXPLANATION

First, recall the following definitions:

  • A binary relation defined on a set is symmetric if for all we have In other words, if a pair belongs to a symmetric relation, then the pair should also be in the relation.

  • A binary relation defined on a set is antisymmetric if for all we have In other words, only one of the pairs, either or can be in an antisymmetric relation. Note that this condition allows pairs of the form

Our relation is defined on Notice that:

  • and

  • and

  • and

There are no other pairs of the form with in the relation. So, for all we have

Therefore, the relation is antisymmetric.

An Equivalent Definition of Symmetricity
?

A binary relation defined on a set is symmetric if for all we have

We can form an equivalent definition by taking the contrapositive of this statement as follows:

However, since and are simply labels of two elements in we usually swap them when writing down our equivalent definition. This gives

Let's see an example where we must use this equivalent definition.

Adjacency Matrices of Symmetric and Antisymmetric Relations
?

Now, let's describe the properties of adjacency matrices for symmetric and antisymmetric relations.

  • A finite binary relation is symmetric if and only if its adjacency matrix is symmetric about the main diagonal (from top-left to bottom-right).

    For example, the relation defined on is symmetric, and its adjacency matrix is the following:

  • A finite binary relation is antisymmetric if and only if its adjacency matrix has no pairs of 's that are symmetric over the main diagonal.

    For example, the relation defined on is antisymmetric, and its adjacency matrix is the following:

    Let's look at any two cells that mirror each other across the main diagonal (that is, the cells and with ). In an antisymmetric relation, we'll never see in both of these cells at the same time. Instead, each mirrored pair must be either one of the following:

    • Both entries are (for example, the purple pair and ).

    • One entry is and the other is (for example, the blue pair and , and the red pair and ).

Plots of Symmetric and Antisymmetric Relations
?

Plots of symmetric and antisymmetric relations have the following properties:

  • A binary relation is symmetric if and only if its plot is symmetric about the line

    For example, the relation defined on is symmetric, and its plot is symmetric about as shown below:

  • A binary relation is antisymmetric if and only if its plot contains at most one point from each pair of the points symmetric about the line

    For example, the relation defined on is antisymmetric.

    Notice that the plot contains the point but not the point and does not have any other pairs of symmetric about points.

Digraphs of Symmetric and Antisymmetric Relations
?

Digraphs of symmetric and antisymmetric relations have the following properties:

  • A binary relation is symmetric if and only if for every arrow belonging to its digraph, the corresponding inverse arrow also belongs to the graph.

    For example, the relation defined on is symmetric.

    Notice that its graph contains both arrows and and has no other arrows of the form with

  • A binary relation is antisymmetric if and only if for all possible and with at most one of the arrows (either or but not both) belongs to the digraph.

    For example, the relation defined on is antisymmetric.

    Notice that the graph contains the arrow but does not contain and it contains but does not contain Also, it has no other arrows of the form with

Example: Identifying Symmetric and Antisymmetric Relations From a Diagram
?

Which of the above digraphs represents a relation, defined on the set that is neither symmetric nor antisymmetric?

EXPLANATION

First, recall the following definitions:

  • A binary relation defined on a set is symmetric if for all we have In terms of a digraph, if we have an arrow then the arrow must be in the digraph too.

  • A binary relation defined on a set is antisymmetric if for all we have So, only one of the arrows, either or can be in the digraph of an antisymmetric relation. Note that this condition allows loops.

With that in mind, let's examine our digraphs.

  • Digraph I represents a symmetric relation since for every arrow belonging to the graph, the corresponding inverse arrow also belongs to the graph.

  • Digraph II represents an antisymmetric relation since for all possible and with at most one of the arrows (either or but not both) belongs to the digraph.

  • Digraph III represents neither a symmetric nor an antisymmetric relation. It's not symmetric since the arrow belongs to the digraph while the arrow does not. It is not antisymmetric since, for example, arrows and belong to the digraph.

Therefore, the correct answer is "III only."

KEY PREREQUISITE
Graphical Representations of Relations

Example: Symmetric and Antisymmetric Over Infinite Sets
?

Which of the following relations are symmetric?

  • if and only if defined on

  • if and only if defined on

  • if and only if defined on

EXPLANATION

First, recall the following definitions:

  • A binary relation defined on a set is symmetric if for all we have In other words, if a pair belongs to a symmetric relation, then the pair should also be in the relation.

  • A binary relation defined on a set is antisymmetric if for all we have In other words, only one of the pairs, either or can be in an antisymmetric relation. Note that this condition allows pairs of the form

With that in mind, let's examine our relations.

  • Relation is not symmetric. Notice that

    • since while

    • since

  • Relation is symmetric. Indeed, if then Notice that for all

  • Relation is symmetric. Indeed, if then Notice that for all

Therefore, the correct answer is " and only."
